steps.title: Identify what the evidence tells us
steps.content:
The passage says that Mayan words of Mixe-Zoquean origin were borrowed during the Olmec-dominant period, and "all those words derive from the Zoque branch." This means the Zoque branch existed as a distinct branch already during Olmec dominance — implying the family had split before that era.
  • "North Central Mixe and the other languages of the Mixe branch likely supplanted the languages of the Zoque branch sometime before 1500 BCE.": Introduces a "supplanting" claim the passage never mentions; the Zoque branch still exists today. ✗
  • "the language of the Olmec civilization was likely the founding language of the family that includes Mayan languages.": Mayan is explicitly "otherwise unrelated" to Mixe-Zoquean; this reverses the passage's logic. ✗
  • "the language of the Olmec civilization contributed words not only to Mayan languages but also to other languages in the Mixe-Zoquean family.": The passage only discusses Mayan borrowings, not contributions within the Mixe-Zoquean family itself. ✗
  • "the Mixe-Zoquean family had already diverged into the Mixe and Zoque branches by the time the Olmecs became the prevailing power in the region.": Directly follows from the evidence: a distinct Zoque branch had to exist before Olmec dominance began for all borrowed words to come from it. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the inference
steps.content: If the family had not yet diverged when the Olmecs dominated, there would be no separate "Zoque branch" from which Mayan words could derive. The fact that borrowed words trace exclusively to Zoque proves divergence preceded Olmec dominance. That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
